body_txt: Uh-oh. You had a roll in the hay with the Farmer's Daughter , her old man found out, and now you and Daisy Mae are standing at the altar in First Church of Bumpusville, with a rose in your buttonhole and a nice long (and loaded) shotgun at your back. There's no getting out of this. No Big Damn Heroes are going to rush in and rescue you. No one is going to interrupt your nuptials at the climactic moment . You've made this bed and now you're going to lie in it . For the rest of your life. At least Daisy Mae isn't that bad looking. I mean, she still has at least half of her teeth... The traditional Shotgun Wedding (replete with gun-toting relatives) is a common staple of comedies set in rural, "hillbilly" areas . Any big-city fellers who wander into such areas had best be discreet about any "minglin'" they do with the local womenfolk, lest they find themselves being forced to stay a lot longer than they had intended. And God help them if the farmer's daughter gets knocked up in that one-night stand. The groom may be shot before he makes it to the altar. Female main characters must also take care when journeying into these kinds of areas, lest an affair force them to stay as well. Sometimes mutual attraction isn't even required before our hero or heroine winds up frog-marched to the chapel. One of the local boys or girls may take a shine to a traveling protagonist and attempt a forced marriage through false accusations. In cases like these, the Big Damn Heroes are much more likely to jump in and save the day . But if you got into this situation by sowing your wild oats a little too freely , you're pretty much on your own. Nowadays, a "shotgun wedding" seldom involves actual firearms; the phrase is a euphemistic way to refer to any couple that gets married because they're expecting a baby . (When characters in older media talk about "having" to get married, this is what they mean). The couple's parents may not be incensed nor the prospective groom unwilling, but there is still an element of compulsion, since the couple may not have married so soon — or at all — if not for the unexpected pregnancy. In fact, if an actual shotgun is involved, the unwilling groom can have the marriage annulled since contracts signed under duress are unenforceable, and "they put a gun to my head" is the textbook definition of duress. This kind of activity was a lot more common back when there was more of a stigma attached to unwed parents , especially a single mother. The biggest reason for these marriages was to preserve her reputation. note It's also, unsurprisingly, Older Than Shotguns , and used to be known as a " knobstick " wedding. But in this day and age, single moms are viewed much more sympathetically and it's the father who's judged if he knocks a girl up then skips town . However, he can remain in good graces without marrying her if he's an active co-parent, and it's widely agreed today that it's better for the parents not to stay together if they're only in it for the kids and not their own relationship. Nonetheless, this trope is still Truth in Television for more socially conservative parts of the world. If the wedding effectively happened when the groom (or bride) wasn't looking, it's an Accidental Marriage . Unsurprisingly, many an Awful Wedded Life story involves a couple that was pressured into marrying due to an unplanned pregnancy, although examples of this trope where the couple end up Happily Married do exist as well. This trope is also related to Altar the Speed (if they want to have the ceremony before the bride's pregnancy starts to show) and Honorable Marriage Proposal (if the groom is marrying her to preserve her reputation, whether he's the father or not). May end in Babies Ever After and can lead to the couple later believing they Married Too Young . Invoking this trope is usually the purpose of The Baby Trap (and frequently, by extension, a Fake Pregnancy ). Also compare and contrast Captive Date , where one side of a romantic evening would rather not be there, but isn't allowed to leave. See also Post-Child Marriage , for when the wedding happens after the birth of the child. An inversion of this is Break Up Demand . Not to be confused with the 2023 film Shotgun Wedding .
